1: The "seven angels with the seven last plagues" and the statement that "with them God's wrath is completed" (etelesthe ho thymos tou theou) positions Revelation 15-16 as the climax and completion of the divine judgment sequence. The word teleo (complete, finish) is the same root as "It is finished" (tetelestai, John 19:30) -- the completion of wrath corresponds to the completion of redemption. The seven plagues as "the last" signals that the bowl sequence outranks the seals and trumpets in finality.
2: The "sea of glass mixed with fire" develops the sea-of-glass image from Revelation 4:6 (the crystalline expanse before the heavenly throne, which itself echoes Exodus 24:10 -- the pavement of sapphire under God's feet -- and Ezekiel 1:22 -- the firmament like crystal over the living creatures). The fire mixed with the glass indicates that this heavenly sea is now charged with judgment (fire = divine judgment throughout Revelation; Revelation 8:5, 7, 8; Revelation 9:17-18). Those standing victorious beside the sea have passed through the fiery trial of persecution without being consumed.
3: Singing "the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb" explicitly identifies the redeemed's position with the Israelites at the Red Sea (Exodus 15:1-18 -- the Song of Moses after the Exodus). The content echoes Psalms 111:2 ("Great are the works of the LORD"), Jeremiah 10:7 ("Who would not fear you, O King of the nations?"), Psalms 86:9 ("All the nations you have made shall come and worship before you"), Deuteronomy 32:4 ("The Rock, his work is perfect"). The Exodus was the paradigmatic salvation event; the final redemption fulfills and surpasses it. The "servant of God" title echoes Numbers 12:7, Deuteronomy 34:5, Joshua 1:2 -- Moses the covenant mediator as the type of the ultimate Mediator.
4: "Who will not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy (hosios), and all nations will come and worship before you, for your righteous acts have been revealed." The question "who will not fear?" echoes Jeremiah 10:7 and Psalms 86:9, and the universal worship of all nations fulfills the eschatological vision of Isaiah 2:2-4 (all nations streaming to Zion), Isaiah 45:23 (every knee bowing), and Psalms 22:27-29 (all ends of the earth turning to the LORD). The term hosios for God -- unusual where hagios is more common -- may echo Psalms 145:17 LXX or Deuteronomy 32:4 (the God of faithfulness).
5: The "temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven" (ho naos tes skenes tou martyriou) specifically invokes the wilderness tabernacle -- "tabernacle of testimony" is the phrase used for the portable sanctuary in Exodus 38:21, Numbers 1:50, 53; Revelation 9:15; Revelation 17:7-8; Acts 7:44. The tabernacle contained the ark of the testimony (ark of the covenant with the stone tablets), hence "testimony" = the tablets/covenant. The opening of the heavenly temple to release the judgment-angels echoes Revelation 11:19 (the heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark). The heavenly sanctuary is the reality of which the earthly tabernacle was the copy (Hebrews 8:5; Revelation 9:11, 23-24).
6: The seven angels come out "clothed in pure, bright linen, with golden sashes around their chests" -- the priestly vestments of Exod 28 (linen garments for the priests) and the description of the heavenly figure in Ezekiel 9:2-3 (a man clothed in linen with a writing case) and Daniel 10:5 (the man clothed in linen with a belt of Uph gold). Linen symbolizes purity (the priestly material, Exodus 28:5-8; Leviticus 16:4, 23) and heavenly status. The golden sashes echo the golden belt of the enthroned figure in Revelation 1:13 (the one like a son of man) -- the judgment-angels share the vestment-character of the glorified Christ.
7: The "seven golden bowls filled with the wrath of God who lives forever and ever" echoes the golden temple vessels of the Jerusalem sanctuary (Exodus 25:29-30; Numbers 4:7; 1 Kings 7:48-50) -- the holy vessels now filled with divine wrath rather than incense of worship. The golden bowls in 5:8 held "the prayers of the saints" as incense; now they hold the wrath that is the answered prayer of Revelation 6:10 ("How long, O Lord?"). The divine title "who lives forever and ever" (to zonti eis tous aionas ton aionon) is the doxological formula from Daniel 4:34; Revelation 12:7 -- the eternal God whose wrath, like his life, is ultimate and inexhaustible.
8: The temple filled with smoke "from the glory of God and from his power" so that "no one could enter the temple until the seven plagues were completed" echoes two OT theophanic scenes: (1) the inauguration of the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35) and temple (1 Kings 8:10-11), when the glory cloud filled the sanctuary so that even Moses/the priests could not enter; (2) Isaiah's throne vision (Isaiah 6:4), where the temple fills with smoke. The exclusion of entry until judgment is complete mirrors the Day of Atonement restriction (Leviticus 16:17 -- no one in the tabernacle while the high priest made atonement) -- the cosmic Day of Atonement must run its course before the temple of divine presence is accessible again.
